name: Leadership Communication
description: Develop the communication skills of effective leaders — public speaking, active listening, team communication, conflict resolution, negotiation, cross-cultural communication, and leading with influence.

modules.title: What Is Leadership?
modules.objectives: Distinguish major leadership theories; compare trait-based and situational leadership models; articulate the principles of servant leadership.
modules.readingNote: Khan Academy — Career Development: Leadership Styles Overview
modules.videoNote: TED Talk — Simon Sinek: How Great Leaders Inspire Action
modules.video2Note: TED-Ed — What Makes a Great Leader?
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Career Development: Leadership self-reflection exercises
modules.assignment: Research one real-world leader (historical or contemporary). Identify which leadership theory best describes their style (transformational, servant, situational, etc.) and write a one-page analysis with at least three supporting examples from their career.

modules.title: Self-Awareness & Emotional Intelligence
modules.objectives: Define the five components of emotional intelligence (EQ); practice self-regulation strategies; apply empathy in leadership scenarios; interpret and act on feedback.
modules.readingNote: Khan Academy — What Is Emotional Intelligence?
modules.videoNote: TED Talk — Daniel Goleman: Why Aren't We More Compassionate?
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Social-emotional learning: self-awareness activities
modules.assignment: Complete a personal EQ self-assessment (rate yourself on self-awareness, self-regulation, motivation, empathy, and social skills, each 1–10). Write a reflection on your two lowest scores: what situations trigger weaknesses, and what specific strategies will you practice to improve over the next month?

modules.title: Public Speaking Foundations
modules.objectives: Structure a speech with clear introduction, body, and conclusion; apply effective delivery techniques including pacing and pausing; use body language and vocal variety; manage speaking anxiety.
modules.videoNote: TED Talk — Chris Anderson: TED's Secret to Great Public Speaking
modules.video2Note: Crash Course Business - Soft Skills — Public Speaking
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Presentation and public speaking practice prompts
modules.assignment: Prepare and record a 2–3 minute speech on a topic you care about. Use the preparation outline template (hook, three main points, conclusion). Review your recording and self-evaluate on: structure, eye contact, pace, filler words, and vocal variety. Write a half-page improvement plan.

modules.title: Storytelling for Leaders
modules.objectives: Identify the elements of a compelling narrative arc; craft a personal leadership story; transform data into an engaging narrative; use storytelling to inspire and persuade audiences.
modules.readingNote: Khan Academy — How to Tell a Great Story
modules.videoNote: TED Talk — Andrew Stanton: The Clues to a Great Story
modules.video2Note: TED-Ed — How to Tell Compelling Stories
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Narrative and persuasive communication exercises
modules.assignment: Write a 300–500 word personal leadership story using the narrative arc (situation, conflict, turning point, resolution, lesson). Then take one statistic or data point relevant to your community and rewrite it as a 2–3 sentence narrative that makes the data emotionally compelling.

modules.title: Active Listening & Empathetic Communication
modules.objectives: Identify barriers to effective listening; practice paraphrasing and reflective listening; read and use nonverbal cues; demonstrate empathetic responses in conversations.
modules.videoNote: TED Talk — William Ury: The Walk from 'No' to 'Yes' (listening & empathy)
modules.video2Note: Crash Course Business - Soft Skills — Active Listening
modules.assignment: Conduct a 10-minute listening exercise with a partner (family member, friend, or classmate). Apply three active listening techniques: full attention (no phone), paraphrasing after each point, and asking at least two open-ended follow-up questions. Afterward, write a reflection on what you heard that you might have missed with passive listening.

modules.title: Team Communication & Collaboration
modules.objectives: Explain communication dynamics within teams; identify common team roles and their communication styles; describe how psychological safety enables honest communication; build trust through consistent communication practices.
modules.videoNote: TED Talk — Amy Edmondson: How to Turn a Group of Strangers into a Team
modules.video2Note: Crash Course Business - Soft Skills — Teamwork Skills
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Collaboration and group dynamics exercises
modules.assignment: Think of a team project you have participated in (school, sport, or community). Map the communication breakdown: where did misunderstandings occur, who was silent when they should have spoken, and how could psychological safety have helped? Write a one-page diagnosis and propose three concrete team communication norms that would improve future projects.

modules.title: Giving & Receiving Feedback
modules.objectives: Apply the SBI (Situation-Behavior-Impact) model to deliver specific, actionable feedback; frame feedback for a growth mindset; receive criticism without defensiveness; create a feedback-friendly team culture.
modules.readingNote: Khan Academy — Giving and Receiving Feedback
modules.videoNote: TED Talk — LeeAnn Renninger: The Secret to Giving Great Feedback
modules.video2Note: Crash Course Business - Soft Skills — Giving Feedback
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Professional communication: feedback practice scenarios
modules.assignment: Write three pieces of feedback using the SBI model for three different hypothetical workplace scenarios (one peer, one direct report, one supervisor). For each, explain what impact the feedback is designed to have and predict how the recipient might respond. Then write a paragraph on a time you received difficult feedback — how did you react, and how would you handle it differently today?

modules.title: Conflict Resolution & Difficult Conversations
modules.objectives: Identify the five conflict-handling styles (Thomas-Kilmann); apply interest-based negotiation to separate positions from interests; use de-escalation language in tense conversations; plan and execute a difficult conversation.
modules.videoNote: TED Talk — Daniel Shapiro: To Resolve a Conflict, First Decide — Are You Willing To?
modules.video2Note: Crash Course Business - Soft Skills — Conflict Resolution
modules.practiceNote: Khan Academy — Conflict and negotiation practice scenarios
modules.assignment: Choose a real or hypothetical workplace conflict (e.g., disagreement over project direction, schedule conflict, performance issue). Write out the full conversation script using de-escalation language, separating positions from underlying interests. Identify which conflict style is being used by each party and explain how switching styles could shift the outcome.

questions.question: Which leadership theory argues that effective leaders adjust their style based on the readiness and skill level of their followers?
questions.answer: Situational Leadership
questions.explanation: Situational Leadership (Hersey & Blanchard) holds that no single leadership style is best; effective leaders diagnose follower readiness and adapt their approach accordingly.

questions.question: A leader who prioritizes the needs of their team members above their own authority and focuses on helping others grow is best described as a:
questions.answer: Servant leader
questions.explanation: Servant leadership (Robert Greenleaf) places the leader in a supportive role — serving the needs of followers first to empower them to perform at their best.

questions.question: According to Daniel Goleman, which component of emotional intelligence involves understanding and sharing the feelings of others?
questions.answer: Empathy
questions.explanation: Empathy is the ability to sense and understand the emotions of others. Goleman identified it as a core EQ competency essential for effective leadership.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "Which of the following is a strategy for managing speaking anxiety before a public presentation" is: Deep breathing and visualizing a successful performance.
questions.answer: True
questions.explanation: True. Deep breathing and visualizing a successful performance is correct.

questions.question: The narrative arc in storytelling typically includes which sequence of elements?
options: Hook, thesis, evidence, call to action
questions.answer: Situation, conflict, turning point, resolution
questions.explanation: A compelling narrative arc establishes the context (situation), introduces a problem (conflict), shows a change (turning point), and delivers the outcome or lesson (resolution).

questions.question: Which of the following best describes active listening?
options: Waiting silently for your turn to speak
Paraphrasing, asking follow-up questions, and giving full attention to the speaker
Nodding and smiling while thinking about your response
Taking detailed notes without looking at the speaker
questions.answer: Paraphrasing, asking follow-up questions, and giving full attention to the speaker
questions.explanation: Active listening requires complete mental presence, reflective paraphrasing to confirm understanding, and follow-up questions that demonstrate genuine engagement with the speaker's message.

questions.question: Psychological safety on a team is best defined as:
options: Freedom from physical harm in the workplace
A belief that team members can speak up without fear of punishment or humiliation
A policy that prevents all conflict between teammates
Ensuring only positive feedback is shared
questions.answer: A belief that team members can speak up without fear of punishment or humiliation
questions.explanation: Amy Edmondson's research defines psychological safety as the shared belief that the team is safe for interpersonal risk-taking — essential for innovation, learning, and honest communication.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "The SBI feedback model stands for:" is: Subject, Behavior, Impact.
questions.answer: False
questions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: Situation, Behavior, Impact.

questions.question: Which of the following is NOT a barrier to effective listening?
options: Preparing your rebuttal while they speak
questions.answer: Maintaining eye contact
questions.explanation: Maintaining eye contact is an active listening technique that demonstrates engagement. The other options — prejudging, mentally rebutting, and distraction — all block genuine listening.

questions.question: Which conflict-handling style involves finding a middle-ground solution where both parties give up something to gain something?
questions.answer: Compromising
questions.explanation: Compromising (Thomas-Kilmann model) involves each party partially satisfying their concerns through mutual concessions — a middle-ground approach that is moderately assertive and cooperative.

questions.question: When delivering feedback to a leader who is more senior than you, which approach is most effective?
options: Frame feedback as a question or observation and focus on shared goals
Send the feedback anonymously to avoid conflict
Wait for a performance review cycle to share your concerns
questions.answer: Frame feedback as a question or observation and focus on shared goals
questions.explanation: Upward feedback is most effective when framed diplomatically — using questions ("I noticed X, could we explore Y?") and connecting concerns to shared organizational goals rather than personal criticism.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "Which component of EQ refers to the ability to control disruptive emotions and impulses" is: Self-regulation.
questions.answer: True
questions.explanation: True. Self-regulation is correct.

questions.question: What does body language primarily communicate during a public speech?
options: The speaker's confidence, credibility, and emotional state
The accuracy of the speaker's data
questions.answer: The speaker's confidence, credibility, and emotional state
questions.explanation: Research suggests that nonverbal cues — posture, gestures, facial expressions, and movement — significantly shape audience perceptions of a speaker's confidence and credibility.

questions.question: Describe the difference between trait leadership theory and situational leadership theory. Give one specific example of how each theory would explain a leader's success or failure.
questions.answer: Trait theory holds that effective leaders possess innate qualities (e.g., charisma, intelligence, decisiveness). Situational theory argues that leadership effectiveness depends on matching style to follower readiness. Example for trait: A naturally charismatic speaker inspires a demoralized team. Example for situational: A leader switches from directive to delegating style as a new employee gains skills.
questions.explanation: Trait theory is leader-centric (fixed characteristics), while situational theory is follower-centric and adaptive. Neither alone fully explains leadership effectiveness.

questions.question: A teammate gives you blunt, critical feedback on a presentation you worked hard on. Using what you know about receiving feedback and emotional intelligence, describe how you would respond in the moment and how you would process it afterward.
questions.answer: In the moment: thank the person, avoid defensiveness, ask a clarifying question to ensure understanding. Afterward: reflect privately on whether the feedback is accurate, separate emotion from the content, identify one actionable change, and follow up with the feedback-giver to show growth.
questions.explanation: Receiving feedback well requires self-regulation (managing the immediate emotional sting), empathy (seeing from the giver's perspective), and growth mindset (treating feedback as information, not attack).

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "Simon Sinek's 'Start With Why' concept argues that great leaders inspire action primarily by:" is: Explaining what they do in detail.
questions.answer: False
questions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: Communicating their core purpose and belief first.

questions.question: A leader who consistently keeps promises, admits mistakes, and shares information openly is building which foundation of team trust?
questions.answer: Integrity-based trust
questions.explanation: Integrity-based trust is built through honesty, consistency, and transparency. It differs from competence trust (skill-based) and benevolence trust (caring about others' welfare).

questions.question: In Hofstede's cultural dimensions, a country with a high Power Distance score is characterized by:
options: Flat organizational hierarchies and direct communication with superiors
Acceptance of unequal power distribution and formal, hierarchical communication
Strong preference for individual achievement over group harmony
High tolerance for ambiguity and uncertainty
questions.answer: Acceptance of unequal power distribution and formal, hierarchical communication
questions.explanation: High Power Distance cultures (e.g., Malaysia, Philippines) accept inequality in power and expect formal deference to authority. Low Power Distance cultures prefer flatter, more egalitarian structures.

questions.question: BATNA in negotiation stands for:
options: Best Alternative To a Negotiated Agreement
Baseline Agreement Terms and Negotiations Approach
questions.answer: Best Alternative To a Negotiated Agreement
questions.explanation: BATNA (Fisher & Ury, Getting to Yes) is your fallback option if negotiations fail. Knowing your BATNA gives you a clear walk-away point and strengthens your negotiating position.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "Which of Cialdini's six principles of influence is demonstrated when a person complies with a request because they feel obligated after receiving a favor" is: Reciprocity.
questions.answer: True
questions.explanation: True. Reciprocity is correct.

questions.question: During a crisis, which communication principle is most critical in the first 24 hours?
options: Waiting for all the facts before saying anything
Responding quickly with what is known, acknowledging uncertainty, and expressing empathy
questions.answer: Responding quickly with what is known, acknowledging uncertainty, and expressing empathy
questions.explanation: Crisis communication experts agree that speed, transparency, and empathy in early messaging are decisive. Silence or delay is often interpreted as guilt or indifference.

questions.question: An effective meeting agenda should include:
options: Topics, time allocations, and owners for each agenda item
Only items that require unanimous approval
A detailed recap of the previous month's results
questions.answer: Topics, time allocations, and owners for each agenda item
questions.explanation: A well-designed agenda assigns each topic a time block and a responsible owner, helping participants prepare, keeping the meeting focused, and establishing accountability for outcomes.

questions.question: In principled negotiation (Getting to Yes), which of the following is a core principle?
options: Make the other party feel they lost something
Use emotional pressure to gain concessions
questions.answer: Separate people from the problem
questions.explanation: Fisher & Ury's principled negotiation has four principles: separate people from the problem, focus on interests not positions, generate options for mutual gain, and insist on objective criteria.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "High-context cultures (e.g., Japan, China) typically communicate by:" is: Stating messages explicitly and directly.
questions.answer: False
questions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: Relying heavily on context, nonverbal cues, and shared understanding.

questions.question: A leader who influences a colleague to support a new initiative by connecting it to that colleague's core values and career goals is using which influence strategy?
questions.answer: Inspirational appeals
questions.explanation: Inspirational appeals (Yukl's influence tactics) involve linking a request to the target's values, ideals, or aspirations. This is particularly effective when the request aligns with the person's deeper motivations.

questions.question: Interest-based negotiation differs from positional bargaining because it:
options: Focuses on what each party demands rather than why
Explores the underlying needs behind each party's stated position
Relies on a mediator to decide the outcome
questions.answer: Explores the underlying needs behind each party's stated position
questions.explanation: Positional bargaining locks parties into stated positions. Interest-based negotiation explores the why behind positions, often revealing shared or compatible interests that enable creative, win-win solutions.

questions.question: Which facilitation technique is most effective for drawing out quiet participants in a group discussion?
options: Calling on them repeatedly until they respond
Using round-robin turns or direct open questions addressed to them
Having them lead the next meeting instead
questions.answer: Using round-robin turns or direct open questions addressed to them
questions.explanation: Round-robin ensures everyone has a structured turn to speak. Direct open questions ("What's your take on this, [name]?") invite participation without putting individuals on the spot uncomfortably.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "Which of the following is an example of the 'Avoiding' conflict style" is: Refusing to discuss the issue and changing the subject.
questions.answer: True
questions.explanation: True. Refusing to discuss the issue and changing the subject is correct.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of the 'hook' at the opening of a leadership presentation?
options: To provide the agenda for the presentation
To introduce all speakers by name
To immediately capture the audience's attention and create curiosity
To summarize the conclusion before the presentation begins
questions.answer: To immediately capture the audience's attention and create curiosity
questions.explanation: A strong hook (story, surprising statistic, provocative question) grabs attention in the first 30 seconds. Audiences decide very quickly whether a speaker is worth listening to.

questions.question: In Brené Brown's research on vulnerability and leadership, she argues that vulnerability is:
options: A weakness that undermines leadership credibility
The birthplace of innovation, creativity, and connection
Only appropriate in personal, not professional, settings
A sign of low emotional intelligence
questions.answer: The birthplace of innovation, creativity, and connection
questions.explanation: Brown's research shows that leaders who embrace vulnerability — uncertainty, risk, and emotional exposure — build deeper trust, foster creativity, and create more authentic connections with their teams.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the 'Collaborating' conflict style?
options: One party wins and the other loses
Both parties give up something to meet in the middle
Both parties work together to find a solution that fully satisfies everyone's concerns
One party gives in to avoid further conflict
questions.answer: Both parties work together to find a solution that fully satisfies everyone's concerns
questions.explanation: Collaborating (Thomas-Kilmann) is both assertive and cooperative. It seeks a win-win outcome by exploring concerns deeply and finding creative solutions — the most constructive style when the issue and relationship both matter.

questions.question: True or False: The answer to "A leader in a cross-cultural meeting notices that her Japanese counterpart never disagrees directly but often says 'That may be difficult.' She should interpret this as:" is: Agreement with minor reservations.
questions.answer: False
questions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: A polite way of expressing disagreement or concern.

questions.question: When making data compelling through storytelling, a leader should:
options: Present as many statistics as possible to appear credible
Lead with the emotion or human impact behind the data before presenting numbers
Avoid using stories — data alone is most persuasive
Read the data directly from a slide without commentary
questions.answer: Lead with the emotion or human impact behind the data before presenting numbers
questions.explanation: Data alone rarely moves people to action. Embedding numbers in a human story — showing what they mean for real people — activates both rational and emotional engagement, making the data memorable and motivating.

questions.question: You are a team leader and two members of your team are in an escalating conflict that is affecting team morale. Using the principles of conflict resolution and difficult conversations covered in this course, describe step-by-step how you would intervene. Include which conflict style(s) you would encourage and why.
questions.answer: Step 1: Meet with each party separately to understand their perspective and underlying interests (not just positions). Step 2: Identify shared interests or goals. Step 3: Bring both parties together in a neutral setting; establish ground rules (no interruptions, respectful language). Step 4: Use de-escalation language and guide both toward interest-based negotiation. Step 5: Encourage the Collaborating style (assertive + cooperative) to find a solution satisfying both parties. Step 6: Document agreed actions and follow up. Avoid Avoiding or Accommodating as these leave the root conflict unresolved.
questions.explanation: Effective conflict intervention separates people from the problem, focuses on interests over positions, and steers toward collaborative outcomes rather than suppressing or bypassing the conflict.

questions.question: Reflect on your leadership communication capstone presentation. Identify two specific communication skills you improved over this course, explain how your presentation demonstrated each skill, and describe one area you still want to develop as a communicator and leader.
questions.answer: Answers will vary. Strong responses will: name two specific skills with course vocabulary (e.g., 'active listening — I incorporated audience questions into my delivery'; 'SBI feedback — I used it to structure my peer feedback sessions'); cite a specific moment from the capstone presentation as evidence; and identify a genuine growth area with a concrete next step (e.g., 'I still struggle with pacing under pressure — I plan to join a Toastmasters club to practice').
questions.explanation: This question assesses metacognitive reflection and genuine self-awareness — hallmarks of high emotional intelligence and a growth mindset.

quizQuestions.question: Which leadership theory holds that effective leaders are born with innate qualities such as charisma and intelligence?
quizQuestions.answer: Trait Theory
quizQuestions.explanation: Trait Theory posits that certain individuals are natural leaders because they possess inherent qualities. Critics note it ignores context and learned behaviors.

quizQuestions.question: Servant leadership, as defined by Robert Greenleaf, prioritizes:
options: The growth and well-being of followers
quizQuestions.answer: The growth and well-being of followers
quizQuestions.explanation: Greenleaf's servant leader serves others first, enabling their development and performance rather than seeking personal authority.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "Which leadership theory holds that effective leaders are born with innate qualities such as charisma and intelligence" is: Trait Theory.
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. Trait Theory is correct.

quizQuestions.question: Self-awareness in emotional intelligence refers to:
options: Knowing the emotions of others before they express them
Accurately recognizing your own emotions and how they affect your behavior
Controlling your emotions so others cannot see them
quizQuestions.answer: Accurately recognizing your own emotions and how they affect your behavior
quizQuestions.explanation: Self-awareness is the foundation of EQ — without knowing your own emotional patterns, you cannot regulate them or understand their impact on your leadership.

quizQuestions.question: A leader who pauses before responding to an angry email, chooses words carefully, and avoids retaliating is demonstrating:
quizQuestions.answer: Self-regulation
quizQuestions.explanation: Self-regulation is the ability to manage disruptive emotions and impulses, think before acting, and maintain composure under pressure.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is the best example of empathy in a leadership context?
options: Telling a struggling employee to simply work harder
Understanding a team member's personal challenges and adjusting expectations temporarily
Keeping your own emotions hidden at all times
quizQuestions.answer: Understanding a team member's personal challenges and adjusting expectations temporarily
quizQuestions.explanation: Empathy means recognizing and understanding the feelings of others and taking their perspective into account when making decisions.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "Self-awareness in emotional intelligence refers to:" is: Accurately recognizing your own emotions and how they affect your behavior.
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. Accurately recognizing your own emotions and how they affect your behavior is correct.

quizQuestions.question: Which component of a speech is responsible for establishing credibility and previewing the main points?
quizQuestions.answer: Introduction
quizQuestions.explanation: An effective introduction grabs attention with a hook, establishes the speaker's credibility, states the thesis, and previews the structure of the speech.

quizQuestions.question: Vocal variety in public speaking refers to changes in:
quizQuestions.answer: Pitch, pace, volume, and tone
quizQuestions.explanation: Vocal variety keeps the audience engaged and helps emphasize key points. A monotone voice loses audience attention even when the content is strong.

quizQuestions.question: Research on nonverbal communication in speeches suggests that:
options: Words alone carry 90% of the message
Body language and tone significantly influence audience perception of credibility
Gestures are a distraction and should be minimized
Eye contact is less important than slide content
quizQuestions.answer: Body language and tone significantly influence audience perception of credibility
quizQuestions.explanation: Studies (Mehrabian and others) show that nonverbal cues — posture, gestures, facial expression, and vocal tone — powerfully shape how a speaker is perceived.

quizQuestions.question: What is the primary purpose of using storytelling in a leadership communication?
options: To entertain the audience and fill time
To create emotional connection and make ideas memorable
To avoid using data and evidence
quizQuestions.answer: To create emotional connection and make ideas memorable
quizQuestions.explanation: Stories activate the brain's neural coupling — listeners experience the story as if it happened to them. This creates emotional resonance and makes the message far more memorable than facts alone.

quizQuestions.question: The 'conflict' element of a narrative arc serves to:
options: Create tension that makes the audience care about the outcome
State the moral of the story
quizQuestions.answer: Create tension that makes the audience care about the outcome
quizQuestions.explanation: Conflict is the engine of a story. Without a problem, challenge, or obstacle, there is no reason for the audience to keep listening. Conflict creates stakes and emotional investment.

quizQuestions.question: When making data compelling through storytelling, a leader should:
options: Lead with raw numbers and let the audience interpret them
Humanize the data by showing its real-world impact on specific people
Avoid using stories with data to maintain objectivity
Use as many statistics as possible to appear credible
quizQuestions.answer: Humanize the data by showing its real-world impact on specific people
quizQuestions.explanation: Data is most persuasive when embedded in a human story. Saying '3 million people went hungry' is less powerful than telling one specific person's story of food insecurity.

quizQuestions.question: A personal leadership story is most effective when it:
options: Focuses only on the leader's successes and achievements
Shows vulnerability, a real challenge faced, and the lesson learned
Keeps all personal details vague and professional
Is rehearsed word-for-word to avoid mistakes
quizQuestions.answer: Shows vulnerability, a real challenge faced, and the lesson learned
quizQuestions.explanation: Authentic stories that include struggle and genuine emotion build trust and relatability. Audiences connect more to honest imperfection than polished perfection.

quizQuestions.question: Paraphrasing during a conversation demonstrates that you:
options: Have been listening and understand the speaker's message
Are ready to move on to your own response
quizQuestions.answer: Have been listening and understand the speaker's message
quizQuestions.explanation: Reflective paraphrasing confirms understanding and shows the speaker they have been heard — one of the most powerful active listening techniques.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following is a common barrier to effective listening?
options: Formulating your rebuttal while the speaker is still talking
quizQuestions.answer: Formulating your rebuttal while the speaker is still talking
quizQuestions.explanation: Planning your response while the speaker is still talking is called 'pseudo-listening.' You appear attentive but miss significant parts of the message.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "Paraphrasing during a conversation demonstrates that you:" is: Have been listening and understand the speaker's message.
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. Have been listening and understand the speaker's message is correct.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "Which of the following is a common barrier to effective listening" is: Maintaining open body language.
quizQuestions.answer: False
quizQuestions.explanation: False. The correct answer is: Formulating your rebuttal while the speaker is still talking.

quizQuestions.question: Psychological safety in a team is most directly associated with which positive outcome?
options: Increased willingness to take interpersonal risks, share ideas, and admit mistakes
Stricter adherence to rules and procedures
quizQuestions.answer: Increased willingness to take interpersonal risks, share ideas, and admit mistakes
quizQuestions.explanation: Google's Project Aristotle found psychological safety was the #1 predictor of high-performing teams — enabling members to speak up, take risks, and learn from errors without fear.

quizQuestions.question: Which team communication role is responsible for ensuring all voices are heard and the discussion stays on track?
quizQuestions.answer: The facilitator
quizQuestions.explanation: The facilitator manages the process of discussion — distributing speaking time, drawing out quiet members, and keeping the team focused on objectives rather than contributing content.

quizQuestions.question: A communication norm that improves team trust is:
options: Only the leader speaks in meetings
Team members are encouraged to challenge ideas, not people
Disagreements are handled in private, never in team settings
All communication goes through the team leader before reaching teammates
quizQuestions.answer: Team members are encouraged to challenge ideas, not people
quizQuestions.explanation: Separating ideas from people creates a safe environment for debate. This norm encourages intellectual challenge without personal attack, which is essential for innovation.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "Psychological safety in a team is most directly associated with which positive outcome" is: Increased willingness to take interpersonal risks, share ideas, and admit mistakes.
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. Increased willingness to take interpersonal risks, share ideas, and admit mistakes is correct.

quizQuestions.question: The 'Situation' in the SBI feedback model refers to:
options: A general description of the employee's overall performance
The specific context or event where the observed behavior occurred
The speaker's personal feelings about the behavior
The desired outcome the leader wants to see
quizQuestions.answer: The specific context or event where the observed behavior occurred
quizQuestions.explanation: Anchoring feedback to a specific situation (e.g., 'In yesterday's team meeting') prevents the recipient from dismissing it as vague or unfair generalization.

quizQuestions.question: Growth-oriented feedback is distinguished from critical feedback primarily because it:
options: Focuses on behaviors that can be changed and frames improvement as possible
Is delivered only in written form
quizQuestions.answer: Focuses on behaviors that can be changed and frames improvement as possible
quizQuestions.explanation: Growth-oriented feedback is forward-looking — it identifies specific observable behaviors (not fixed personality traits) and frames the message within the possibility of improvement.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "The 'Situation' in the SBI feedback model refers to:" is: The specific context or event where the observed behavior occurred.
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. The specific context or event where the observed behavior occurred is correct.

quizQuestions.question: The Thomas-Kilmann conflict model assesses conflict styles on two dimensions. What are they?
quizQuestions.answer: Assertiveness and cooperativeness
quizQuestions.explanation: The TKI model plots conflict styles on two axes: assertiveness (concern for own needs) and cooperativeness (concern for others' needs). The five styles result from different combinations of these.

quizQuestions.question: Interest-based negotiation differs from positional bargaining because it:
options: Focuses on what is demanded rather than why
Seeks the underlying needs behind stated positions
quizQuestions.answer: Seeks the underlying needs behind stated positions
quizQuestions.explanation: Positional bargaining ('I want X') is rigid and zero-sum. Interest-based approaches ('Why do you want X?') reveal underlying needs that often have creative, mutually beneficial solutions.

quizQuestions.question: Which of the following phrases is an example of de-escalation language in a difficult conversation?
options: 'You always do this and it needs to stop.'
'I want to understand your perspective — can you help me see it from your side?'
'This is your fault and I have the evidence to prove it.'
'If you don't change, there will be consequences.'
quizQuestions.answer: 'I want to understand your perspective — can you help me see it from your side?'
quizQuestions.explanation: De-escalation language is curious, non-blaming, and invites collaboration. It lowers defensive reactions and moves both parties from opposition toward dialogue.

quizQuestions.question: True or False: The answer to "The Thomas-Kilmann conflict model assesses conflict styles on two dimensions. What are they" is: Assertiveness and cooperativeness.
quizQuestions.answer: True
quizQuestions.explanation: True. Assertiveness and cooperativeness is correct.
